<local_ctx>
def insort : Nat → List Nat → List Nat
  | n, [] => [n]
  | n, x::xs => if n <= x then n::x::xs else x::(insort n xs)

def sorted : List Nat → Bool
  | [] => True
  | [_x] => True
  | x::y::xs => and (x <= y) (sorted (y::xs))
</local_ctx>

<broken_theorem>
theorem (x: Nat) (xs: List Nat) : (sorted xs → sorted (insort x xs)) := by
  intro x xs
  induction xs with
  | nil =>
      intro h
      simp [insort, sorted] at *
  | cons a xs ih =>
      cases xs with
      | nil =>
          intro h
          simp [insort, sorted] at *
      | cons b bs =>
          intro h
          -- use the provided lemma for the general cons-cons case
          simpa using (lemma_hint_2 x a (b :: bs) h)
</broken_theorem>

response:
<reasoning>
The error arises because the theorem already has binders `(x) (xs)`; after `:= by` the goal is `sorted xs → sorted (insort x xs)` (as a Bool equality to `true`), so there are no additional binders to `intro x xs`. We should only introduce the hypothesis `h : sorted xs = true` and then use the provided lemma `lemma_hint_2`, which directly proves the desired statement for any list of the form `y :: ys`. We can handle `xs = []` by simp.
</reasoning>

<lean4_proof>
  intro h
  cases xs with
  | nil =>
      simp [insort, sorted] at *
  | cons y ys =>
      simpa using (lemma_hint_2 x y ys h)
</lean4_proof>
